planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Hosta sieboldii is one of the smaller-growing true hosta species — a parent of many modern landscape hosta cultivars. Spreads by offsets to form clumps.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Hosta sieboldii is a smaller-leaved, lance-leafed Japanese hosta species with characteristic narrowly-edged creamy-white margins on its dark green, wavy-margined leaves — one of the most distinctive and charming of the species hostas, parent of many white-margined cultivars in the modern hosta trade. The lavender flower scapes rise above the foliage in midsummer, topped with funnel-shaped, lightly fragrant flowers. More heat-tolerant than some hostas, making it a good choice for southern gardens in zones 7–9. The white-edged foliage remains attractive even in light shade conditions. A good parent species for understanding the range of hosta diversity — more grass-like and delicate than the massive blue-leaved Siebold hostas. Multiplies readily into clumps that can be divided every 3–4 years for propagation.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Hosta minor is one of the smallest true hosta species, forming tiny clumps suitable for the front of shaded borders, woodland rock gardens, and path edges where its petite scale is an advantage.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Hosta minor is a Korean dwarf hosta species — one of the smallest hostas in the wild, producing tiny, oval, dark green leaves with distinctive wavy margins and very small clumps appropriate for the scale of rock garden features, shaded path edges, and the front of woodland borders. The flowers are tall relative to the leaf mound, producing lavender to purple flower scapes that rise above the foliage in attractive proportions. A plant of rock ledges and cliff margins in the mountains of Korea. As the parent or ancestor of many modern miniature and small hosta cultivars, understanding this species illuminates the full range of hosta diversity. Useful for trough gardens, small shaded terracotta pots, and other situations where the massive clumps of standard landscape hostas would be too large.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Hosta clausa spreads aggressively by stolons compared to most hostas, quickly colonizing large areas in appropriate conditions. Excellent as a shade groundcover for difficult areas.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Hosta clausa is one of the most unusual hostas — a Korean species named for its cleistogamous (closed) flowers that never open fully, staying closed and self-pollinating without releasing pollen or allowing cross-pollination. This botanical curiosity means the flowers, while attractive in bud, never display the characteristic open, funnel-shaped hosta flower. The species compensates with an unusually aggressive spreading habit for a hosta — it sends out stolons to colonize large areas, making it one of the best groundcover hostas for naturalizing in shade gardens. The dark green, medium-sized leaves are attractive and conventional in appearance despite the botanical curiosity of the closed flowers. An excellent choice where spreading shade groundcover is desired without the aggressive nature of English ivy.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to lean, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; quaking grass thrives in lean, slightly alkaline soils typical of chalk downlands, limestone grasslands, and old meadows. Avoid rich, fertile soils which cause excessive leafy growth at the expense of flowering.

directions: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to lean, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; quaking grass is one of the most charming and distinctive ornamental grasses — producing loose, airy panicles of small, flattened, heart-shaped or teardrop-shaped spikelets that dangle on delicate, hair-thin stalks and tremble and 'quake' in the slightest breeze, producing a soft rustling sound. The Latin 'Briza' comes from Greek for 'food-grain,' though this species is not edible. The spikelets are attractive fresh in arrangements and dry beautifully for dried flower work. Native to limestone grasslands and chalk downlands of Europe and western Asia — natural meadow and grassland habitat — so it thrives in lean, slightly alkaline soils and dislikes fertile, enriched garden conditions. Naturalizes slowly in open, lean meadow conditions. The dried seed heads persist attractively through winter. A lovely addition to cottage-style and naturalistic meadow plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, dry to average, sandy, rocky, or peaty, lean soil in partial shade; wavy hairgrass grows naturally in dry, acidic, rocky or sandy woods, moors, and heathlands of the northern hemisphere. More tolerant of dry shade than tufted hairgrass.

directions: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, dry to average, sandy, rocky, or peaty, lean soil in partial shade; wavy hairgrass is a fine-textured, tufted perennial grass of acidic, rocky, open woodland and moorland habitats across the Northern Hemisphere — common in pine barrens, heathlands, and rocky mountain forests of North America and Europe. The extremely fine, thread-like leaves form dense, dark green tufts, and the summer flower panicles are delicate and airy with a distinctive wavy (flexuosa) arrangement of branches. Attractive bronze-purple tints appear in the foliage in autumn. More drought and shade tolerant than tufted hairgrass (D. cespitosa), making it valuable for dry, shaded, acidic sites under conifers and in sandy woodland gardens where few ornamental grasses grow. Valuable for underplanting under pines and rhododendrons. Self-sows modestly in appropriate lean, acidic conditions.
